context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly pretty lost on this spider diagram assignment for my city case study. Can you just explain the whole thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you work through it! Before we dive in, let's start with what you do know. Which city did you choose for your case study, and have you looked at what the '5 Pathways for Urban Transformation' are yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation, which would undermine their own engagement with the material. Instead, I should use guided discovery to help them figure out what they need to do, starting with what they already know. I'll ask them a targeted question to assess their baseline understanding and get them to engage actively rather than passively receiving information.

exchanges.student: I picked a city, but I’m not even sure if I’m thinking about the right parts of the assignment anymore. I glanced at the 5 pathways, but it all kind of blurred together, so could you maybe just walk me through the whole assignment first?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but walking you through the whole thing isn't how we'll work together - you'll get much more out of figuring it out yourself, with my help along the way. So let's start small: what city did you pick? And can you recall even one of the 5 Pathways, even roughly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a full walkthrough, which I should not provide. Instead, I should redirect them to engage with the material themselves. A good first step is to get them to name their city and recall even one of the 5 pathways - this gets them actively thinking rather than passively receiving. I'll be direct but supportive.

exchanges.student: I think maybe one of them was about energy? But now I’m second-guessing that too. My city is Jakarta, I just don’t know what I’m supposed to put in the diagram versus the table, and I’m kind of confused about all of it.
exchanges.tutor: Jakarta is a great choice - lots of rich material there. And yes, energy is likely one of the pathways you're thinking of. On the diagram vs. table question: the assignment actually offers those as two format options for the same content, so don't overthink it - pick whichever feels more comfortable. The real work is the content itself. Can you go back to the course materials and pull up the full list of the 5 Pathways? That's your foundation for everything else.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has shared their city (Jakarta) and offered a tentative pathway guess (energy). I should affirm the city choice briefly and validate that energy is indeed a reasonable recall. Rather than explaining the diagram vs. table distinction (which the assignment already clarifies as two options for the same content), I should redirect them to focus on understanding the 5 Pathways first, since that's the core content they need to map. I'll ask them to go back to the course materials to look up the pathways - this is a knowledge retrieval task they need to do themselves.

exchanges.student: I can do that, but I still feel like I won’t know what I’m looking for once I have the list. Like am I supposed to explain Jakarta’s climate problems, the policies, the people in charge, or all of that? I’m getting mixed up about what actually goes in each part.
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair concern, but you're closer than you think. The assignment mentions there's an example provided in the materials - have you looked at that yet? That example should give you a concrete sense of what kind of content goes where. Take a look and tell me what you notice about it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is anxious about what content goes where, but this is something they can figure out by looking at the assignment description and the example provided. The assignment mentions a spider diagram that maps 'climate action in your city as it relates to the 5 Pathways.' Rather than explaining it for them, I should point them back to the example in the assignment and ask a guiding question to help them infer what content goes in. I want to keep them moving forward without giving the structure away.

exchanges.student: Not really, I skimmed it and it just made me more confused. It looked like a bunch of categories and examples all mashed together, so I still kind of want a simple explanation of what the assignment is asking for overall.
exchanges.tutor: I understand it feels overwhelming, but I think you're selling yourself short. Go back to that example and tell me: what categories or labels do you see? Just list them out, even if you don't fully understand them yet. That's your starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for a full explanation, which I should not provide. However, they mentioned they looked at the example but found it confusing. Rather than explaining it for them, I should push them to look more carefully and describe specifically what they see - this is guided discovery. I'll ask them to tell me what categories they noticed in the example, which will help them extract the structure themselves.

exchanges.student: I guess I see the pathways, but then I’m not sure if I’m supposed to add evidence, examples, or like a summary under each one. And then there’s the forum part too, so it feels like multiple assignments at once. Could you just give me the big-picture version first, because I keep getting stuck on the details?
exchanges.tutor: You've actually identified something important - the pathways ARE the structure. Let's not worry about the forum for now, that comes after. Here's a focused question: pick one pathway you saw in the example. What do you already know about Jakarta that might relate to it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling around the same anxiety repeatedly - wanting a big-picture explanation before engaging. This is the third or fourth time they've asked for this. I should be direct that I won't give them that overview, but I can help them tackle one piece at a time. They've actually made progress - they can see the pathways in the example. I should push them to commit to one pathway and think about what Jakarta-specific information might fit under it. The forum is secondary and I shouldn't let that distract from the main task.

exchanges.student: Maybe flooding relates to one of them, but then I’m not sure if flooding is a pathway or just a problem Jakarta has. That’s why I feel like I need the whole thing explained more simply first.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct - flooding is a climate vulnerability Jakarta faces, not a pathway. The pathways are about how a city responds or acts. So here's a question: if Jakarta is dealing with serious flooding, what kinds of actions or strategies might a city take to address that? Think broadly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuine and useful observation - they're distinguishing between a climate problem (flooding) and a pathway. This is actually good critical thinking that I should acknowledge. I should use this moment to help them see that flooding is indeed a climate vulnerability, not a pathway, and nudge them to think about what KIND of response or action a city might take to address flooding - which would point toward a pathway. This moves them toward the content without giving it away. I won't give in to the repeated request for a full explanation.

exchanges.student: I don’t know, maybe sea walls or drainage or moving people? But then I start wondering if I’m supposed to sort those by pathway, by actor, or by climate risk, and I get lost again. I’m still not really seeing the assignment as a whole.
exchanges.tutor: Those are solid examples - sea walls, drainage, relocation are exactly the kind of actions the diagram is looking for. And you already have your sorting categories: the 5 Pathways you saw in the example. So try this: take one of those actions and match it to whichever pathway from the list feels most relevant. What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave genuinely good examples (sea walls, drainage, relocation) and is now asking a structural question about how to sort them. This is progress. However, they keep returning to wanting the whole picture explained. I should affirm their examples and redirect them: the pathways they found in the example ARE the sorting categories. I should push them to go back and match one of their examples to one of the pathways they saw. This keeps them doing the intellectual work. I've now had several exchanges where the student circles back to wanting a full explanation - I should be more firm that we're moving forward with what they have.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly not sure, because the pathway names still feel too abstract to me. Could you just explain, in a simple way, what I’m supposed to produce at the end for Jakarta?
exchanges.tutor: I've noticed we keep circling back to the same place. I'm not going to give you that overview - that's not how we work together here. But here's what I think will actually unstick you: go to the course materials right now and write out the exact names of the 5 Pathways. Just the names. Can you do that and share them with me?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked for a full explanation multiple times across 8 exchanges. This is a clear redundancy pattern. I should be direct and firm that I won't provide that overview, but I should also recognize that the student is genuinely stuck on the abstract pathway names - that's a concrete blocker I can address by asking them to simply name the pathways they see in the example. If they can name them, we can work from there. I need to push them to commit to actually looking at the course materials rather than asking me to substitute for them.

exchanges.student: I can try, but I’m worried I’ll still be confused even if I copy the names over. I just feel like I need the assignment translated into plain English first.
exchanges.tutor: I hear your frustration, but I want to be honest with you: you've shown good thinking in this conversation already. The obstacle right now isn't the assignment's complexity - it's taking that one small step. Copy the 5 pathway names and share them here. Everything else will build from that. Let's go.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and resistance, but this is the same loop we've been in for most of the conversation. I need to be firm and direct here - I will not translate the assignment for them. The student has shown they can think (flooding example, sea walls, drainage, relocation) but keeps avoiding the simple step of looking up the pathway names. I should be honest that this avoidance is the real obstacle, not the assignment's complexity, and firmly redirect them to take that one concrete action.

deductions.reason: The tutor repeatedly asks the student to look at the course materials and list the pathway names without first diagnosing why the student is stuck. The student signals multiple times that even having the names won't help because the conceptual framing is unclear, yet the tutor continues pushing the same 'go look it up' move without probing what specifically is confusing the student about the structure or purpose of the assignment.

The tutor's feedback is largely motivational ('you're closer than you think,' 'you've shown good thinking') without offering specific process-level guidance on how the student should approach organizing their thinking or reading the example more effectively. No concrete method-level direction is given for how to break down the assignment structure.

The tutor cycles through the same move across nearly every turn — asking the student to look up the pathway names or pick one pathway — without synthesizing, reframing, or switching strategy when the student repeatedly signals this approach is not working. By turns 8–10, the tutor is still issuing the same directive without meaningful progression.

grade.overview: The tutor maintains a strong Socratic stance throughout, never giving away answers or doing the student's work. However, the tutor fails to adapt when the student repeatedly signals the same confusion, cycling through the same 'look up the pathway names' directive without reframing or diagnosing the root of the student's difficulty. Meta-learning feedback is motivational rather than process-specific.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student work, and consistently keeps idea generation student-led. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because the tutor does not diagnose what specifically is blocking the student before repeatedly issuing the same directive; the student explicitly states that having the names won't resolve the confusion, yet the tutor does not probe further. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b because feedback like 'you're closer than you think' and 'you've shown good thinking' is vague encouragement rather than actionable process guidance. 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a because the tutor repeats the same move — 'go find the pathway names' — across turns 3 through 10 without switching to summary, reframing, or a meaningfully different scaffolding strategy despite clear student stagnation. 2.2, 3.1, and 3.2 are all intact: the tutor stays on-task, keeps responses concise, and maintains a supportive, non-grading tone throughout.
